title: Introducing Yourself
situation: You're meeting your new Dutch neighbors and colleagues for the first time.

phrases.english: Hello, I'm [name]. Pleased to meet you.
phrases.context: 'Aangenaam' = pleased to meet you (lit. 'pleasant'). Slightly formal but universally appropriate. More casual: 'Hoi, ik ben...' or just 'Leuk je te ontmoeten' (nice to meet you, informal)

phrases.hindi: Ik kom uit India. Ik ben hier al twee jaar.
phrases.english: I come from India. I've been here for two years already.
phrases.context: 'Ik kom uit' = I come from + country. 'Al' = already (indicates you've been doing this for some time). Dutch people will ask 'hoe lang ben je hier al?' (how long have you been here)
phrases.pronunciation: ik KOM owt IN-dya. ik ben HEER al TWAY yaar.

phrases.hindi: Ik ben uw/jouw nieuwe buurman/buurvrouw. Ik woon nu op nummer [X].
phrases.english: I'm your new neighbor (m/f). I now live at number [X].
phrases.context: 'Buurman' = male neighbor, 'buurvrouw' = female neighbor. 'Op nummer' = at number. Use 'uw' (formal) for older neighbors, 'jouw' for peers.
phrases.pronunciation: ik ben uw/yow NIOW-we BUUR-man/BUUR-vrow. ik WOHN nu op NUM-mer [X].

phrases.hindi: Wat doe jij? Ik werk bij... Ik studeer...
phrases.english: What do you do? I work at... I study...
phrases.context: 'Wat doe jij?' = what do you do (for work)? 'Ik werk bij' + company/organization. 'Ik studeer' + subject. The Dutch usually ask this early in conversations — it's friendly, not nosy.
phrases.pronunciation: wat DOO yay? ik WERK bay... ik stoo-DAYR...

phrases.hindi: Spreek je ook Nederlands? Ik leer het nog. Mijn Nederlands is niet zo goed.
phrases.english: Do you speak Dutch too? I'm still learning it. My Dutch isn't so good.
phrases.context: Useful honesty phrase. Dutch people will often switch to English when they hear this, but it opens the door to patient Dutch conversation. 'Nog' = still (ongoing). They'll appreciate the effort.
phrases.pronunciation: SPRAYK ye ook NAY-der-lants? ik LAYR het NOK. mayn NAY-der-lants is NEET zo CHOOT.

phrases.hindi: Hoe gaat het met u/jou? Goed, dank je. En met jou?
phrases.english: How are you? Fine, thank you. And you?
phrases.context: Standard greeting after the introduction. 'Met u' (formal) or 'met jou' (informal). 'Goed, dank je / dank u' is the standard answer. Very few Dutch people give a long answer to this question.
phrases.pronunciation: hoo CHAAT het met u/yow? CHOOT, DANK ye. en met YOW?

phrases.hindi: Welkom in de buurt! Als u iets nodig heeft, kom maar aankloppen.
phrases.english: Welcome to the neighborhood! If you need anything, just knock on the door.
phrases.context: What neighbors often say. 'Als u iets nodig heeft' = if you need anything. 'Aankloppen' = to knock (separable: 'kloppen aan'). A warm, neighborly phrase.
phrases.pronunciation: WEL-kom in de BURT! als u EETS NOH-dich HAYFT, KOM maar AAN-klop-pen.

phrases.hindi: Tot ziens! Tot gauw. Dag! Doei!
phrases.english: Goodbye! See you soon. Bye! Bye-bye (informal)!
phrases.context: Farewells: 'Tot ziens' = formal/standard goodbye. 'Tot gauw' = see you soon. 'Dag' = casual day-goodbye. 'Doei' = very casual, like 'bye'. Often said twice: 'Doei doei!'
phrases.pronunciation: tot ZEENS! tot CHOW. DACH! DOO-ee!

grammar_notes: 'Ik kom uit' + country without article: 'ik kom uit Nederland', 'ik kom uit India' — no 'het' or 'de' needed after 'uit' for country names (with a few exceptions like 'de Verenigde Staten')
Professions: no article in Dutch. 'Ik ben dokter' (I am a doctor) — NOT 'ik ben een dokter'. With adjective: 'Ik ben een goede dokter' — then you need 'een'
The informal 'je/jij' vs formal 'u': with new neighbors/strangers your age or younger → 'je/jij'. With older neighbors → start with 'u', they'll tell you if they prefer 'je'
Responding to 'Hoe gaat het?': Dutch people expect a short answer. 'Goed, dank je' is perfect. Long answers about your health will surprise them.
culture_notes: Dutch people value directness — introduce yourself clearly with your name and what you do. Don't be vague
The Dutch are generally private people but friendly to neighbors. Bringing 'stroopwafels' or something small when you move in is appreciated
Dutch small talk topics: weather ('het weer'), cycling, work. Avoid: money, relationship status (initially), politics (unless invited)
Dutch people shake hands when meeting, less hugging/kissing than Southern Europeans

skill_breakdown.explanation: Dutch greetings by time of day: 'Goedemorgen' (until ~12), 'Goedemiddag' (12-18), 'Goedenavond' (after 18). 'Hallo' and 'Hoi' work any time. 'Aangenaam' or 'Leuk u/je te ontmoeten' after the name exchange.

practice_prompt: You are Anna, the user's new Dutch colleague, meeting them at the koffiecorner on their first day. Use informal 'je/jij' throughout — peers at a Dutch workplace default to 'je' once introduced, and 'u' would feel oddly distant. Open verbatim with: 'Hoi! Ik geloof dat ik nog niet de kans heb gehad — ik ben Anna, je nieuwe collega. Hoe heet je?' Share that you work in marketing and live in Utrecht-Oost, then ask their name, what they do, where they're from, and how long they've been in Nederland. Weave in 'Ik ben...', 'Waar kom je vandaan?', 'Wat doe je voor werk?', 'Hoe lang ben je hier al?', and 'Aangenaam', mixing present and perfect tenses. Drop one Dutch-cultural tip — a lunch spot, the Albert Heijn nearby, or the gemeente. Flag mistakes with [[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]] — especially 'u' after you've used 'je', V2 word-order errors, and English-style 'ik ben genoemd X' for 'ik heet X'. Stay in Dutch unless asked otherwise. Keep it to 6-8 turns and close with contact exchange or 'aangenaam — tot snel!'
